title: "An endless demand for compute" | Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq
summary: Groq is a hardware chip specifically designed for LLM inference. Jonathan Ross, the founder, discusses Groq's LPU architecture, its comparison to GPUs via Pareto curves and cost-per-token metrics, and a strategic agreement with NVIDIA. The interview covers technical aspects like static scheduling and mixture-of-experts models.
writeup: Julia Turc interviews Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq, about the LPU, a chip built specifically for LLM inference rather than general-purpose computation. The conversation compares the LPU to GPUs using Pareto curves and cost-per-token metrics, and explains design choices such as static scheduling and how the architecture handles mixture-of-experts models. Ross also discusses a strategic agreement with NVIDIA and the premise that demand for inference compute is effectively unbounded.
